name: Watanabe no Tsuna
description: Watanabe no Tsuna, also known as Tsuna-Genji, was a prominent samurai and retainer of Minamoto no Yorimitsu (Raiko), one of the most famous warrior figures in Japanese folklore. He was a member of the elite group known as the Shitennō, the Four Heavenly Kings, who served Yorimitsu. Tsuna was renowned for his incredible strength, bravery, and loyalty, and he is the subject of numerous tales and legends, often involving encounters with demons and supernatural creatures. His most famous exploit involves severing the arm of the demon Ibaraki-dōji at the Rashōmon gate in Kyoto. This act cemented his reputation as a fearless warrior and a protector of the people. Tsuna's stories are often used to exemplify the virtues of a true samurai, including courage, honor, and unwavering dedication to his lord. He is remembered as a powerful figure who stood against the forces of evil and helped to maintain order in a turbulent era. Beyond his martial prowess, Tsuna's tales often explore themes of deception, transformation, and the blurred lines between the human and supernatural realms. His legacy continues to inspire and captivate audiences, ensuring his place as a legendary hero in Japanese folklore. His story is a testament to the enduring power of human courage in the face of overwhelming odds, and he remains a symbol of strength and resilience.
subtitle: Demon Slayer and Loyal Retainer
achievements: Serving as one of the Shitennō
Displaying unwavering loyalty to Minamoto no Yorimitsu
Becoming a symbol of samurai bravery
companions: Other members of the Shitennō (e.g., Sakata no Kintoki, Urabe no Suetake)

keyMyths.title: Severing Ibaraki-dōji's Arm
keyMyths.description: Watanabe no Tsuna encountered the demon Ibaraki-dōji at the Rashōmon gate and, in a fierce battle, severed its arm. The demon fled, leaving its arm behind as a trophy of Tsuna's victory and a testament to his strength.
keyMyths.source: Various folklore collections and theatrical performances

keyMyths.title: The Encounter at Modoribashi Bridge
keyMyths.description: A beautiful woman asked Tsuna for a ride across Modoribashi Bridge. Mid-crossing, she transformed into a demon, but Tsuna, unfazed, forced her to release him and fled. This showcases his ability to withstand supernatural trickery.
keyMyths.source: Various folklore collections and theatrical performances

keyMyths.title: Tsuna's Dream and the Arm
keyMyths.description: After severing Ibaraki-dōji's arm, Tsuna kept it locked away. His aunt visited and requested to see it, only to transform into the demon and steal it back, showing the demon's cunning and Tsuna's momentary lapse in vigilance.
keyMyths.source: Various folklore collections and theatrical performances

sources.author: Helen Craig McCullough
sources.description: A historical epic that provides context for the era and the role of warriors like Tsuna.
sources.text: A historical epic that provides context for the era and the role of warriors like Tsuna.

sources.author: Donald Keene
